title: Derivatives of Polynomials and Exponential Functions
objectives: Apply the Constant Rule to differentiate constant functions
Use the Power Rule to differentiate functions of the form $x^n$ for any real exponent $n$
Apply the Constant Multiple, Sum, and Difference Rules to differentiate polynomial functions term by term
Differentiate the natural exponential function $e^x$ and recognize that it is its own derivative

devotional.title: Wisdom in the Foundations
devotional.content: "By wisdom the Lord laid the earth's foundations, by understanding he set the heavens in place." Just as God used wisdom to establish the orderly structure of creation, we now build our foundation of differentiation rules. The Power Rule reveals an elegant pattern woven into the fabric of mathematics—a pattern that reflects the rational order of the Creator's design.

content.text: Remember computing $\frac{d}{dx}[x^{3}]$ from the limit definition in Chapter 3? It took half a page of expanding $(x+h)^{3}$, canceling terms, and factoring. The answer was $3x^{2}$, and it was correct—but the labor was immense.
Watch this:
One line. The same answer. Welcome to the **Power Rule**.

In this section, we build our first collection of differentiation rules. Each rule is *proved* from the limit definition of the derivative (the definition does the heavy lifting once, so we never have to repeat the work), and once established, it becomes a permanent tool. By the end of this section, you will be able to differentiate any polynomial—and the natural exponential—in seconds.
The Constant Rule
We start with the simplest possible function: a constant.
If $f(x) = c$ for some constant $c$, then the graph of $f$ is a horizontal line. Its slope is $0$ everywhere. Let us confirm this from the definition.
This should feel obvious: a function that never changes has a rate of change of zero. But notice how quickly the limit definition confirms it.
The Power Rule
The Power Rule is arguably the single most useful differentiation formula. Before we state it, let us look at the pattern.
In the previous chapter, we computed the following derivatives from the limit definition:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}[x^{1}] &= 1 = 1 \cdot x^{0}, \\[4pt] \frac{d}{dx}[x^{2}] &= 2x = 2 \cdot x^{1}, \\[4pt] \frac{d}{dx}[x^{3}] &= 3x^{2} = 3 \cdot x^{2}. \end{aligned}$$
The pattern is unmistakable: the exponent comes down as a coefficient, and the new exponent is one less than the original.

content.title: The Power Rule
content.content: If $n$ is any positive integer, then $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[x^{n}\bigr] = n\,x^{n-1}.$$

We use the limit definition and the Binomial Theorem. Recall that $$(x + h)^{n} = \sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k} x^{n-k}\,h^{k} = x^{n} + n\,x^{n-1}\,h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}\,h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}.$$ Therefore, $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[x^{n}\bigr] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{(x+h)^{n} - x^{n}}{h} \\[6pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{x^{n} + n\,x^{n-1}h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n} - x^{n}}{h} \\[6pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{n\,x^{n-1}h + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}}{h} \\[6pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\left[n\,x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2}x^{n-2}h + \cdots + h^{n-1}\right] \\[6pt] &= n\,x^{n-1}. \end{aligned}$$ Every term after $n\,x^{n-1}$ contains a factor of $h$, so those terms all vanish in the limit.

content.text: **Historical Note:** Isaac Newton (1643–1727) and Gottfried Leibniz (1646–1716) both discovered the Power Rule independently in the late 1600s. Newton, a devout Christian who wrote more about theology than mathematics, saw his scientific work as uncovering the rational order God had woven into creation. Leibniz, equally devout, described the universe as the "best of all possible worlds" designed by a perfect Creator. The Power Rule was among the earliest fruits of the calculus they both invented.
The Power Rule as stated applies to positive integers. We will extend it later to all real exponents, but even now it handles an enormous class of functions.

content.title: Rewriting Before Differentiating
content.solution: We first rewrite $g$ using a negative exponent: $g(x) = x^{-4}$. Applying the Power Rule (which we will prove works for negative integers shortly), $$g'(x) = -4\,x^{-4-1} = -4\,x^{-5} = -\frac{4}{x^{5}}.$$

content.text: In fact, the Power Rule holds for *all* real exponents—integer, rational, and irrational. We will prove the rational case using implicit differentiation and the general case using logarithmic differentiation in later sections. For now, we state the general version.

content.title: The Power Rule (General Version)
content.content: If $n$ is any real number, then $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[x^{n}\bigr] = n\,x^{n-1}.$$

content.title: Fractional and Negative Exponents
content.solution:
(a) Write $f(x) = x^{1/2}$. Then $f'(x) = \frac{1}{2}\,x^{-1/2} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$.

(b) $g'(x) = -3\,x^{-4} = -\dfrac{3}{x^{4}}$.

(c) $h'(x) = \pi\,x^{\pi - 1}$. (Yes, $\pi$ is just a constant exponent.)

content.text: The Constant Multiple Rule
What happens if we multiply a function by a constant? Intuitively, multiplying by $c$ stretches the graph vertically by a factor of $c$, which should stretch the slope by the same factor.

content.title: The Constant Multiple Rule
content.content: If $c$ is a constant and $f$ is differentiable, then $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[c\,f(x)\bigr] = c\,f'(x).$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[c\,f(x)\bigr] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{c\,f(x+h) - c\,f(x)}{h} \\[6pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\, c \cdot \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} \\[6pt] &= c \cdot \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} \\[6pt] &= c\,f'(x). \end{aligned}$$ We used the Constant Multiple Law for limits in the third step.

content.text: The Sum and Difference Rules

content.title: The Sum and Difference Rules
content.content: If $f$ and $g$ are both differentiable, then $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[f(x) + g(x)\bigr] = f'(x) + g'(x)$$ and $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[f(x) - g(x)\bigr] = f'(x) - g'(x).$$

We prove the Sum Rule; the Difference Rule follows by writing $f - g = f + (-1)\cdot g$ and applying the Constant Multiple Rule. $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[f(x) + g(x)\bigr] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{\bigl[f(x+h) + g(x+h)\bigr] - \bigl[f(x) + g(x)\bigr]}{h} \\[6pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\left[\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + \frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h}\right] \\[6pt] &= \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{g(x+h) - g(x)}{h} \\[6pt] &= f'(x) + g'(x). \end{aligned}$$ We used the Sum Law for limits in the third step.

content.text: Together, the Constant Multiple, Sum, and Difference Rules tell us that differentiation is a **linear operation**: it respects addition and scalar multiplication. This means we can differentiate any polynomial *term by term*.

content.title: Differentiating a Polynomial
content.solution: We differentiate term by term: $$\begin{aligned} p'(x) &= \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[3x^{5}\bigr] - \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[7x^{3}\bigr] + \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[2x^{2}\bigr] - \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[x\bigr] + \frac{d}{dx}\bigl[4\bigr] \\[6pt] &= 3(5x^{4}) - 7(3x^{2}) + 2(2x) - 1 + 0 \\[6pt] &= 15x^{4} - 21x^{2} + 4x - 1. \end{aligned}$$

content.title: Rewriting Radicals and Fractions
content.solution: First, expand (we will learn the Product Rule in the next section, but for now, we expand): $$g(t) = t^{1/2} \cdot t^{2} + 3\,t^{1/2} = t^{5/2} + 3\,t^{1/2}.$$ Now differentiate term by term: $$g'(t) = \frac{5}{2}\,t^{3/2} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,t^{-1/2} = \frac{5}{2}\,t^{3/2} + \frac{3}{2\sqrt{t}}.$$

content.title: Finding a Tangent Line
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = x^{4} - 6x^{2} + 1$ at $x = 2$.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Find the point.** At $x = 2$, $$y = 2^{4} - 6(2)^{2} + 1 = 16 - 24 + 1 = -7.$$ The point is $(2, -7)$.

**Step 2: Find the slope.** $y' = 4x^{3} - 12x$, so $$y'(2) = 4(8) - 12(2) = 32 - 24 = 8.$$

**Step 3: Write the equation.** Using point-slope form: $$y - (-7) = 8(x - 2) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = 8x - 23.$$

content.text: The Derivative of the Natural Exponential Function
We now turn to a function that is fundamentally different from any polynomial: the natural exponential function $f(x) = e^{x}$.
In a polynomial like $x^{n}$, the variable is the *base* and the exponent is a constant. In $e^{x}$, the base is a constant and the *exponent* is the variable. This difference is profound.

Let us apply the limit definition: $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[e^{x}\bigr] = \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{e^{x+h} - e^{x}}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0}\frac{e^{x}\cdot e^{h} - e^{x}}{h} = e^{x}\cdot\lim_{h \to 0}\frac{e^{h} - 1}{h}.$$ Everything depends on the value of $\lim_{h \to 0}\dfrac{e^{h} - 1}{h}$.

We can estimate this limit numerically: as $h$ approaches 0, the expression $\frac{e^{h} - 1}{h}$ approaches 1. For example, at $h = 0.1$, we get approximately $1.052$; at $h = 0.01$, we get approximately $1.005$; at $h = 0.001$, we get approximately $1.0005$.
The limit is $1$. In fact, this is one of the defining properties of the number $e$: it is the unique base for which $\lim_{h \to 0}\frac{e^{h} - 1}{h} = 1$.

This is one of the most remarkable results in all of calculus: *the natural exponential function is its own derivative*. No other function (aside from constant multiples of $e^{x}$ and the zero function) has this property. The rate at which $e^{x}$ grows is *exactly equal* to its current value.

content.title: The Tangent Line to the Exponential
content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = e^{x}$ at $x = 0$.
content.solution: At $x = 0$: $y = e^{0} = 1$, and $y' = e^{0} = 1$. The tangent line is $$y - 1 = 1(x - 0) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = x + 1.$$ This is the linearization of $e^{x}$ near $x = 0$: for small $x$, $e^{x} \approx 1 + x$.

content.title: Finding Horizontal Tangent Lines
content.problem: Find all points on the curve $y = x^{3} - 12x + 1$ where the tangent line is horizontal.
content.solution:
A tangent line is horizontal when $y' = 0$. We have $$y' = 3x^{2} - 12 = 3(x^{2} - 4) = 3(x-2)(x+2).$$ Setting $y' = 0$ gives $x = 2$ or $x = -2$.

At $x = 2$: $y = 8 - 24 + 1 = -15$. At $x = -2$: $y = -8 + 24 + 1 = 17$.

The tangent line is horizontal at $(2, -15)$ and $(-2, 17)$.

content.text: **Caution: The Power Rule vs. the Exponential Rule**
The Power Rule applies when the *base* is the variable: $\frac{d}{dx}[x^{n}] = nx^{n-1}$.
The Exponential Rule applies when the *exponent* is the variable: $\frac{d}{dx}[e^{x}] = e^{x}$.
Do **not** confuse them! For instance, $\frac{d}{dx}[x^{5}] = 5x^{4}$ (Power Rule), but $\frac{d}{dx}[5^{x}]$ requires techniques we will develop in a later section.
Summary of Rules
Exercises

content.problem: Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = x^{3}$ at the point $(2, 8)$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = \sqrt{x}$ at $x = 9$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $y = e^{x}$ at $x = 1$.
Find all points on the curve $y = 2x^{3} - 3x^{2} - 12x + 5$ where the tangent line is horizontal.
Find the point on the curve $y = x^{2} - 4x + 1$ where the tangent line has slope $6$.
Differentiate $f(x) = (x^{2} + 1)(x^{3} - 2)$ by first expanding the product. (We will learn a faster method in the next section.)
Find the equation of the normal line (perpendicular to the tangent line) to $y = x^{4}$ at the point $(1, 1)$.
If $f(x) = x^{3} + bx^{2} + cx$, find $b$ and $c$ so that $f$ has a horizontal tangent at $(1, 2)$.
Show that the curve $y = e^{x}$ has no horizontal tangent lines.
Find the values of $a$ and $b$ so that the line $y = 2x + b$ is tangent to the curve $y = x^{2} + a$.
Prove the Constant Multiple Rule from the limit definition: if $f$ is differentiable and $c$ is a constant, then $(cf)' = cf'$.
Use the limit definition to prove that $\frac{d}{dx}[x^{4}] = 4x^{3}$ without using the Binomial Theorem (expand $(x+h)^{4}$ directly).
The population of a bacterial colony at time $t$ (in hours) is modeled by $P(t) = 500e^{t}$. Find $P'(2)$ and interpret its meaning in context.
